title: Portfolio Management and Investment Advice

labor_share.method: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 5239 ($166,457,424,125) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 5239 ($454,212,096,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: ancestor 5239. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

occupations.title: Securities, Commodities, and Financial Services Sales Agents
Executive Secretaries and Executive Administrative Assistants

n_total.source: QCEW 2024 private annual avg establishments (SUSB out of scope for this code; establishments are a proxy for firms — overcounts multi-unit firms and includes state-registered micro-RIAs)

n_band.derivation: RESEARCH GAP FILLED. Chain: (1) Source: IAA/COMPLY 2026 Investment Adviser Industry Snapshot (2025 data, published June 2026): 16,544 SEC-registered investment adviser firms. AUM distribution: 67.4% manage <$1B AUM = 11,150 firms; 87.3% manage <$5B AUM = 14,443 firms; 12.7% manage $5B+ AUM = 2,101 firms. Firms in $1B-$5B AUM band: 14,443-11,150 = 3,293 firms. Firms in $500M-$1B AUM band (estimated as ~29% of total from IAA 2024 figure that 58.3% of firms hold $100M-$1B): ~4,831 firms. (2) Revenue conversion: fee rate 0.85% of AUM (Schwab RIA Benchmarking Study 2025: advisers serving individual investors avg $424M AUM; fee rate per Advisor Growth Strategies ~0.85% blended for $500M-$5B tier). (3) EBITDA margin: Schwab benchmarking 2024 median operating margin 27.8%; EBITDA margins for fee-only RIAs 25-40% per multiple benchmarking sources; using 25% as conservative floor. (4) EBITDA per AUM band: $500M-$1B avg $700M AUM → revenue $5.95M → EBITDA $1.49M → IN $1-10M band; $1B-$5B avg $2.3B → revenue $19.6M → EBITDA $4.9M → IN band; $5B-$10B avg $7B → revenue $59.5M → EBITDA $14.9M → OUT (>$10M). (5) In-band count: $500M-$1B (~4,831 firms) + $1B-$5B (~3,293 firms) = ~8,124 at 25% margin. Cross-check with Damodaran 11.65% margin: $500M-$1B EBITDA $693K out; $1B-$5B EBITDA $1.68-$3.96M in; $5B-$10B EBITDA $6.93M in; in-band ~3,293+1,050 = 4,343. Central estimate: 5,000 (midpoint of 4,343-8,124 range). State-registered advisors (<$100M AUM, ~15,000 additional firms) excluded — their EBITDA clearly below $1M floor regardless of margin assumption. Quality ESTIMATE.
n_band.margin_source: Damodaran Jan 2026: Investments & Asset Management EBITDA/Sales 11.65%

current_adoption_pct.source: Schwab RIA AI Research Study 2026 (press release, Charles Schwab, 2026)
current_adoption_pct.figure_quoted: 63% of RIAs now use AI tools in some capacity; only approximately 10% are fully integrating AI into their business strategy; 82% of current users rely on generative AI through individual experimentation rather than firm-wide systems. 'Material use' defined as systematic AI embedding in core operations (not just personal notetaking apps). Estimated material/embedded adoption ~20% (between 10% full integration and 63% any use; calibrated to firm-wide deployment threshold).

historical_analogs.source: Robo-advisor adoption trajectory at RIAs; broader fintech platform adoption in wealth management (multiple industry sources)
historical_analogs.figure_quoted: Robo-advisor/digital advice adoption at RIAs: from <5% offering digital tools in 2012 to ~50% of larger RIAs offering some digital capability by 2019, approximately 7 years. Schwab 2026 study notes AI adoption 'more than doubled since 2023' — indicating rapid acceleration suggesting faster adoption than robo wave. Blended analog: 7 years (slightly faster than robo due to AI being embedded natively in software platforms like Orion, Salesforce Financial Services Cloud, Redtail, etc.).

owners_60plus_pct.source: Cerulli Associates US Advisor Metrics (press releases and public data); IAA/COMPLY 2025 Investment Adviser Industry Snapshot; Commonwealth/Cerulli press release 2024
owners_60plus_pct.figure_quoted: 37% of financial advisors anticipate retiring within the next decade (Cerulli), implying they are currently aged ~58-68; advisors 55+ control 36.9% of assets and 39.2% of headcount. Average retirement age 68. Interpreting '37% retiring in 10 years' as a proxy for 60+ ownership share among firm owners/principals: 0.37. Succession readiness gap: 1-in-4 advisors planning to retire lack a defined succession strategy (Cerulli).

succession_shortage_documented.evidence: Cerulli: '37% of financial advisors collectively controlling $10.4 trillion (40% of industry assets) are expected to retire within the next decade' and 'more than one-quarter (26%) of advisors who anticipate retiring within the next decade are unsure of their succession plans.' FP Transitions and DeVoe succession-readiness studies widely document advisor succession shortage. Documented shortage confirmed.

active_consolidators.source: DeVoe & Company Q3 2025 Deal Book; ctacquisitions.com RIA M&A Multiples 2026; FINTRX 2024 Year in Review
active_consolidators.figure_quoted: Focus Financial, Wealth Enhancement Group, Mercer Advisors, Mariner, Beacon Pointe, Hightower, Creative Planning, Captrust, Cerity Partners, Corient (CI Financial), Allworth, Pathstone, Modern Wealth, Wealthspire (Madison Dearborn), EP Wealth, Sequoia Financial, Carson Group, Savant, Savant, and others — each reporting multiple transactions in 2024-2025. Top 10 acquirers accounted for 46-52% of 272+ announced RIA transactions in 2025. Conservative count of 20 PE-backed platforms actively acquiring.

buy_mult.source: ctacquisitions.com RIA Wealth Management M&A Multiples 2026 (citing DeVoe & Company, ECHELON Partners, Advisor Growth Strategies Deal Room); DeVoe Q2 2025 Deal Book
buy_mult.figure_quoted: $100M-$250M AUM sellers: 6.5x-8.5x adjusted EBITDA (2024-Q2 2026); $250M-$500M AUM sellers: 7.5x-10.0x adjusted EBITDA. Median for $100M-$500M AUM band: ~7.5x adjusted EBITDA. Note: 'adjusted EBITDA' in RIA context = EBITDA normalized for market-rate advisor compensation (EBOC + owner comp normalization), consistent with template rule 3. Sub-$100M AUM on SDE basis 4.5-7.0x SDE converted to EBITDA equivalent ~6-9x. Primary sources: DeVoe & Company RIA Deal Book (cited); ECHELON Partners RIA M&A Deal Report (cited). EBITDA basis confirmed.

exit_mult.source: ctacquisitions.com RIA Wealth Management M&A Multiples 2026 (citing DeVoe, ECHELON, Focus Financial/CD&R take-private); ctacquisitions.com who buys RIAs 2026
exit_mult.figure_quoted: $1B+ AUM platform exits: 11.0x-16.0x adjusted EBITDA (2024-Q2 2026); $5B+ AUM: 13.0x-18.0x. Focus Financial/CD&R take-private at ~15x LTM adjusted EBITDA cited as public-market reference. 2025 median deal valuation: 11.6x adjusted EBITDA (record per DeVoe). For platform-quality exits ($10M+ EBITDA, $1B+ AUM), central estimate 13.5x (midpoint of 11-16x range, consistent with the broader 2025 market at 11.6x median trending higher for large platforms). EBITDA basis confirmed.

pricing_structure.value: AUM-based fee (~1% of AUM for retail/HNW wealth management, declining toward 0.85% blended for larger accounts); flat annual fee/retainer growing as alternative (~15-20% of firms offering fee-only planning subscriptions); no cost-plus billing
pricing_structure.source: Schwab RIA Benchmarking Study 2025; IAA/COMPLY 2025 Investment Adviser Industry Snapshot; ctacquisitions.com RIA valuation guide 2026
pricing_structure.figure_quoted: Fees are priced as ~1% of AUM for retail accounts; blended realized fee rates declining toward 0.85% as accounts scale. RIA expenses reached 82% of revenue in 2023 (Fidelity). AUM fees decouple pricing from cost — AI savings do not automatically flow to clients. Planning-fee-only and subscription models (e.g., $200-$500/month) represent growing but minority share. No cost-plus or hourly billing is standard for AUM RIAs.

breakdown_by_role.rationale: Financial-plan drafting, meeting prep/notes (AI meeting-scribes live in the RIA stack — Otter.ai, Jump, Zocks), proposal generation and client reporting automate heavily and are already being deployed in 2026. Trust relationship, behavioral coaching, investment-committee accountability, and rainmaking do not automate. 25% reflects the plan-drafting/reporting/prep fraction of advisor time — the prompt guidance suggests 0.2-0.35.

breakdown_by_role.role: 41-3031 Securities, Commodities, and Financial Services Sales Agents
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Brokerage-side contamination from 3-digit proxy (note: role_mix is LOW quality, 3-digit 523 bleed). For hybrid RIA/BD reps present in true 523940 firms: same split as advisors but higher pure-sales weight makes trust/relationship a larger fraction. Prospect research, quote preparation, and paperwork automate; actual relationship-building and closing do not. 20% conservative for this role.

Research notes, screening, rebalancing, performance attribution, and model-portfolio reporting are heavily AI-tractable (much already rules-based; AI upgrades quality and speed). Final allocation judgment and investment-committee accountability persist. Model-portfolio outsourcing (TAMPs) has been shrinking this role for a decade independent of AI. 30% reflects the research/screening/attribution fraction.

Compliance-adjacent reporting, AUM fee calculation, billing reconciliation, and operations dashboards automate substantially. Supervisory principal duties (SEC/state registration obligations, compliance oversight) require named humans. 30% reflects automatable reporting/billing/ops fraction.

Strategic decisions, regulatory oversight, client relationship management persist. However, management reporting, budgeting, and operational analytics automate well. 25% reflects the reporting/analytics fraction.

Highly routine document processing and data entry. Account opening/transfers (ACAT paperwork), RMD processing, and scheduling automate near-fully. 80% fraction consistent with highly routine administrative work.

Routine client inquiries (account balance, statement requests, basic transaction status) automate via AI voice/chat agents. Complex/emotional client situations (market downturns, estate events) require human. 80% for routine-query-dominant CSR role in RIA context.

Reconciliation, performance reporting, and fee billing automate. Regulatory filings (Form ADV, audits) require human judgment and sign-off. 40% reflects the reconciliation/reporting automation fraction.

Small RIAs buy rather than build; this role is concentrated at larger asset managers within the 3-digit proxy. AI coding assistants accelerate development but do not substitute core software engineering judgment. 20% reflects code review, documentation, and boilerplate generation.

breakdown_by_role.role: 43-6011 Executive Secretaries and Executive Administrative Assistants
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Scheduling, meeting prep, travel coordination, and correspondence drafting automate substantially with AI. Complex judgment calls (gatekeeping priorities, sensitive communications) require human. 65% reflects high automation potential for this role.

breakdown_by_role.role: Unlisted roles (ops staff, client-service specialists, compliance officers, portfolio managers, CCO, advisor associates)
breakdown_by_role.rationale: The unlisted tail (28.66% of wage bill at 3-digit 523 level) contains: (a) operations/client-service staff (~12-15% of total) — ACAT transfers, money movement, CRM hygiene, scheduling — highly automatable (~70%); (b) compliance officers/CCO (~3-5%) — Advisers Act compliance, SEC exam prep — partially automatable for reporting/monitoring (~25%); (c) portfolio managers/investment staff not separately listed (~5-8%) — rebalancing, performance attribution partially automatable (~30%); (d) advisor associates/client service (~5-8%) — meeting prep, financial plan maintenance — automatable (~30%). Blended: 0.55×0.70 + 0.15×0.25 + 0.15×0.30 + 0.15×0.30 = 0.385+0.038+0.045+0.045 = 0.513, adjusted down to 0.35 to reflect uncertainty in tail composition and the non-automatable human-judgment elements (compliance oversight, fiduciary accountability).

pi_dist.rationale: AUM fee pricing (~1% of AUM) fundamentally decouples revenue from cost. Unlike cost-plus/hourly billing, AI savings on operations and planning prep do NOT mechanically flow to clients as lower fees — the 1% fee is anchored by the value-of-advice proposition, not the cost of delivery. This is a strong pi_dist structural advantage. However, fee compression exists: robos established a 25-30bps floor for pure allocation; AI-native advice could pressure the 1% standard for mass-affluent clients. Realized fee rates have trended down modestly (~5-10bps per year across the industry per Schwab benchmarking). The founding-advisor portability risk (books can leave with advisors) means client relationships are partially person-held rather than firm-held, reducing effective pi_dist. Scored 0.60: strong AUM-fee decoupling structure is the primary positive; fee compression risk and advisor portability moderate downward.
sources: Fidelity Wealth Advisor Solutions 2023 benchmarking (expenses 82% of revenue)

pi_moat.rationale: SEC/state RIA registration with named human fiduciary creates regulatory moat; Advisers Act Section 205 requires client consent for advisory contract assignment on acquisition (real integration cost). Behavioral trust and long-term relationship — decades of evidence that cheap automated alternatives (index funds, robos) grew human advice rather than killing it. Complex wealth situations (tax, estate, behavioral coaching) persist. However: (1) advisory books are famously portable — the advisor, not the firm, owns the client in most RIA contexts; (2) AI is the first substitute capable of holding a conversation and providing personalized guidance, qualitatively different from static robos; (3) mass-affluent base may migrate to AI-native platforms over 10-20 years, bifurcating the market. Scored 0.55: strong near-term structural protection (fiduciary regulation, trust, complexity), partially offset by medium-term AI substitution risk for simpler relationships and advisor portability risk.
sources: Advisers Act (SEC) registration and fiduciary requirements

t50_years.derivation: Current material/embedded AI adoption = 20% (estimated from Schwab 2026 study: 63% any use, 10% fully integrated, ~20% systematic embedded use). Target = 50%. Gap = 30pp. Historical analog: robo-advisor adoption at RIAs — from <5% in 2012 to ~50% digital capability at large RIAs by 2019 = ~7 years. AI is moving faster than robos: Schwab notes adoption 'more than doubled since 2023' (i.e., ~3pp/year historically implied, but current acceleration implies ~8-10pp/year near-term). AI is natively embedded in RIA platforms (Orion, Salesforce FSC, Redtail, Jump) so adoption is partially automatic as firms upgrade software. Derivation: 30pp gap at 5pp/year average pace = 6 years (2026→2032). The faster pace vs. robos justified by platform-embedded deployment; moderated by compliance inertia and firm-wide policy requirements (only ~10% currently fully integrated). Range: 4-9 years.

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: uplift_pp = 11.81pp. A = 1.67 (< 5), so the cross-check 1 tension condition (uplift < 3pp while A >= 5) does not apply. The moderately slow adoption pace (t50=6 years) is internally consistent with the higher V score: the automatable fraction (~30% of labor cost) is genuinely large in this knowledge-work industry, but firm-wide embedding of AI requires navigating compliance inertia, fiduciary liability, and client-consent requirements that slow the pace beyond individual tool adoption.

terminal_value.justification: Two decades of cheap automated alternatives (index funds, robos) grew AUM-based human advice rather than killing it — fiduciary trust, behavioral accountability, and estate/tax complexity are structural demand anchors. AI cuts the advisor's costs meaningfully (ops, planning prep, reporting — real and large, V=4.72 reflects this). However, AI is qualitatively different from robos: it can hold a conversation, personalize guidance, and will improve continuously. The most plausible long-run scenario is market bifurcation: high-net-worth advice (complex, relationship-intensive, estate/tax/behavioral) remains durable; mass-affluent simple allocation migrates to AI-native platforms over 10-20 years. This bifurcation reduces aggregate addressable market over the long run without eliminating the premium segment. PRESSURED: demand shrinks but persists — the HNW/complex segment is durable, the mass-affluent base is at risk from AI-native alternatives over the next 10-20 years.

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: V derived from OEWS 3-digit 523 wage shares (LOW quality, noted) and within-role task fractions based on analysis of automatable vs. judgment/relationship tasks in the wealth management RIA model. A derived from Schwab 2026 AI adoption survey (current material use ~20%) and robo-advisor historical adoption analog (7 years to 50%). No reference to PE deal volume, RIA acquisition counts, or consolidation trends used for V or A. Confirmed.
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: Billing mix: ~80-85% of revenue is AUM-based fees (~1% of assets); ~15-20% flat fee/retainer/subscription growing. AUM fees structurally decouple price from cost — AI savings on back-office operations and planning prep do NOT flow to clients as lower fees under this model; the operator retains the efficiency gain. Fee compression risk exists (robo pressure, AI-native competition) but is a competitive/market-pricing phenomenon, not a mechanical billing-structure pass-through. pi_dist = 0.60 reflects the strong structural fee-decoupling (better than cost-plus) partially offset by fee compression risk and advisor portability (books portable with departing advisors). pi_moat = 0.55 reflects fiduciary regulation and trust barriers offset by AI substitution risk over the medium term.

top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: At t50=4 years A=2.5 vs A=1.67 at t50=6; S moves from 3.62 to ~3.97 at t50=4. At t50=9, A=1.11 and S~3.29. The 4-9 year range spans S from ~3.29 to ~3.97, a ±10% swing. AI's pace of embedding in RIA software platforms (Orion, Salesforce FSC, etc.) is the key driver — if platform-embedded AI counts as firm adoption, t50 could be as short as 4 years.

n_band drives TD via log10(n_band)/4. At n_band=3,500, TD=0.886 → B~7.27 → S~3.57. At n_band=8,000, TD=0.976 → B~7.77 → S~3.68. Relatively modest impact on S given TD already near 1.0 in both cases. The bigger uncertainty is the margin assumption (Damodaran 11.65% → n_band~4,343; Schwab 25% → n_band~8,124).

Driven by the unlisted tail (28.66% at 0.35 fraction) and advisors (21.27% at 0.25). At ARS=0.22, V_raw=0.086, V=3.44, S~3.30; at ARS=0.40, V_raw=0.156, V=6.25, S~3.94. The PRESSURED terminal-value rating caps upside — if ARS drives S much higher, that tension would need explanation.

M = clamp(4*(13.5-buy)/buy, 0, 10). At buy=6.0, M=5.0, S~3.93. At buy=9.5, M=1.68, S~3.23. The buy_mult is the most sensitive M input; buyer competition (20+ PE-backed aggregators bidding competitively) may have already compressed small-firm entry multiples toward the upper end of the range.

reviewer_note: Heterogeneous code warning: 523940 (2022 NAICS) merges portfolio management (old 523920 — institutional asset managers, hedge fund managers, mutual fund advisers) with investment advice (old 523930 — wealth managers, financial planners, RIAs). Scored on the DOMINANT sub-segment by firm count: wealth-advice RIAs (~80%+ of firm count, ~50% of revenue by best estimate given institutional AUM concentration at top firms). The role_mix is at 3-digit 523 (LOW quality), bleeding in brokerage-side occupations (41-3031 at 15.45% wage share likely overstates its presence in pure 523940 firms). n_band = 5,000 is a researched ESTIMATE with wide range (3,500-8,000); this fills the documented data gap per V3_PLAN.md 3.1 exception. Dataset n_total = 88,068 (QCEW establishments) massively overcounts — SEC-registered RIAs total ~16,544 (2025 IAA Snapshot); state-registered sub-$100M advisors add ~15,000 more firms. The labor_share (0.3907) computed at ancestor 5239 level is LOW quality; true 523940 labor share likely higher (labor-intensive advisory business) or lower (institutional asset managers have high non-labor profit margins). The CONTROL-CODE WARNING on M has been addressed: buy_mult (7.5x) and exit_mult (13.5x) sourced explicitly from DeVoe, ECHELON, and Advisor Growth Strategies as named in the prompt trap, with EBOC/EBITDA normalization stated. Confidence = LOW given LOW-quality role_mix, LOW-quality labor_share, ESTIMATE n_band, and the heterogeneous code blending materially different economics.
